# Tariademor

*A world generated by Cartogenesis v0.13.0 from seed `aurelia-9`. Regenerate it exactly with `node src/cli.ts generate --seed aurelia-9`.*

## Overview

- **Extent:** 360 × 360 cells
- **Land / ocean:** 33.7% land, 66.3% ocean, 2 lake(s)
- **Climate & life:** dominant biome **Savanna**, 16 biomes in all
- **Peoples:** 20 regions, 13 settlements
- **Capital:** Niantetomor, seat of House Mantetindo
- **Realms:** 15 rose over the simulated centuries; 2 stand in the present year, Rauriabinte the greatest among them
- **The present year:** 1100 A.T. — 1100 years after the Thaw
- **The calendar:** year 0 is **The Long Winter**; every date in this book is counted after the Thaw and wears `A.T.`
- **Highest peak:** 5,527 m · **volcanoes:** 3 (0 active)

## Volcanoes

- **Mount Storkahrei** — *the white hill* — stratovolcano, dormant, summit 5,283 m, **caldera** cradling a crater lake
- **Mount Vollahrei** — *the storm hill* — cinder cone, extinct, summit 4,056 m
- **Mount Vynbry** — *the old stone* — cinder cone, dormant, summit 2,501 m

## Notable features

- **Mount Mantebeosso** — the highest peak, *the white mountain*
- **The Knauskba** — the greatest river, *the bright spring*
- **Lake Mantemilen** — the largest inland water, *the white water*

- Deoda is a coast of rolling grassland, a country of vineyards and old stone towns, seat of Nianteliria.
- In Beossocaria, bare grey peaks and little else; a hard country the southern tongue softens in the telling.
- In Shinsham, golden savanna and little else but the sea; its ports paved with other countries' coin.
- In Khirarer, golden savanna and little else but the sea; its people pearl-divers and salt-traders.
- In Beossoriando, bare grey peaks and little else; a hard country the southern tongue softens in the telling.
- In Khirkhidku, golden savanna and little else but the sea; its ports paved with other countries' coin, seat of Khirkad.
- Midshaa is a shore of golden savanna — where the caravans meet the tide, and both leave richer.
- Braba is an inland reach of windswept scrub, herders of the high pastures, sparing with words.
- Stagrbei is a shore of windswept scrub — its people reckoning wealth in boats and grudges, seat of Stagragrull.
- Shinrarer is a coast of golden savanna, its ports paved with other countries' coin, seat of Ghashakhukha.
- Mairkhidku is a country of dry basin country — its people caravan-traders and readers of stars.
- Peonariando is a shore of rolling grassland — terraced hills running down to busy water, seat of Deotelen.
- Midkhaim is a country of golden savanna — well-keepers and star-readers, rich in patience.
- Brasturn is a country of bare grey peaks — where hospitality is law because the weather enforces it.
- Jakhaim is a country of golden savanna — a country crossed at night and remembered by its wells.
- Khirzash is a shore of golden savanna — its ports paved with other countries' coin, seat of Khirkhukha.
- Midrarer is a shore of golden savanna — its ports paved with other countries' coin.
- Deoriando is a shore of rolling grassland — terraced hills running down to busy water, seat of Deotomor.
- In Deopeona, rolling grassland and little else but the sea; a country of vineyards and old stone towns, seat of Peonatomor.
- Pananeosso is a coast of rolling grassland, its harbours louder than its temples, seat of Niantetomor.

## Ruling houses

### House Vassosena — realm of Tariademor

*Vassosena, the red thorn; Tariademor, the gold home.*

- **71–90** Semorir Vassosena
- **91–110** Tindai Vassosena
- **111–140** Tindai II Vassosena the Unlucky
- **141–181** Riandir Vassosena
- *… 30 rulers over the intervening years …*
- **1017–1031** Senai II Vassosena
- **1032–1062** Veollir Vassosena
- **1063–1089** Tariai Vassosena the Cruel
- **1090–1100** Saunai Vassosena *(reigning)*

### House Mantetindo — realm of Lossocaria

*Mantetindo, the white blood; Lossocaria, the dread land.*

- **81–116** Raurian Mantetindo
- **117–133** Dorian Mantetindo
- **134–154** Dintan Mantetindo
- **155–174** Niantan Mantetindo the Golden
- *… 31 rulers over the intervening years …*
- **1005–1032** Variai Mantetindo
- **1033–1045** Peonai Mantetindo the Wise
- **1046–1078** Dintai Mantetindo the Learned
- **1079–1100** Tindan Mantetindo the Unlucky *(reigning)*

## Notable figures

- **Peonir, the Explorer** — Peonir charted the far coasts and traced the Knauskba to its source.
- **Peoriai, the General** — Peoriai broke the long siege of Niantetomor.
- **Niantir, the Architect** — Niantir raised the great road between Niantetomor and Deotomor.
- **Belenai, the Scholar** — Belenai mapped the waters of Mantemilen.
- **Tindan, the Mountaineer** — Tindan was first to stand atop Mantebeosso and return alive.

## Settlements

### Cities

- **Niantetomor**, *the green fort* (**capital**, port, trade hub, rich) — Livestock, Grain, Horses, Fish *(founded 100)*
- **Deotomor**, *the sea fort* (port, prosperous) — Wine, Grain, Livestock, Horses *(founded 155)*

### Towns

- **Deotelen**, *the sea market* (port, prosperous) — Grain, Livestock, Fish, Horses *(founded 201)*
- **Nianteliria**, *the green haven* (port, trade hub, rich) — Grain, Livestock, Wine, Iron *(founded 271)*
- **Khirtomor**, *the sea fort* (port, prosperous) — Livestock, Fish, Grain, Horses *(founded 299)* — formerly **Khirkad**, before conquest remade the name
- **Stagrlamor**, *the sea hall* (port, prosperous) — Livestock, Horses, Wine, Grain *(founded 338)* — formerly **Stagragrull**, before conquest remade the name

### Villages

- **Deodemor**, *the sea home* (port, prosperous) — Livestock, Stone, Wine, Iron *(founded 352)*
- **Stagrdinte**, *the sea spring* (port, prosperous) — Grain, Horses, Livestock, Timber *(founded 437)* — formerly **Stagrba**, before conquest remade the name
- **Khirpana**, *the sea field* (port, modest) — Livestock, Horses *(founded 460)* — formerly **Khirkhukha**, before conquest remade the name
- **Peonatomor**, *the grass fort* (prosperous) — Livestock, Grain, Horses, Wine *(founded 521)*
- **Deonelen**, *the sea bridge* (port, prosperous) — Fish, Livestock, Grain, Horses *(founded 571)*
- **Midliria**, *the grass haven* (prosperous) — Livestock, Fish, Stone, Horses *(founded 650)* — formerly **Midshu**, before conquest remade the name

## Ruins

*Settlements that did not survive to the present day.*

- **Ghashakhukha** (village) — founded 572, abandoned in 629

## Names remade by conquest

*Where a foreign power held a land long enough, its towns took new names — the old land-word kept, the settlement re-said in the ruler's tongue.*

- **Khirkhukha** → **Khirpana** *(the sea field)* — Kesh (desert) land under Meridian (southern) rule, c. 174
- **Ghashakhukha** → **Ghashtomor** *(the river fort)* — Kesh (desert) land under Meridian (southern) rule, c. 174
- **Midshu** → **Midliria** *(the grass haven)* — Kesh (desert) land under Meridian (southern) rule, c. 174
- **Khirkad** → **Khirtomor** *(the sea fort)* — Kesh (desert) land under Meridian (southern) rule, c. 224
- **Stagragrull** → **Stagrlamor** *(the sea hall)* — Auld (northern) land under Meridian (southern) rule, c. 474
- **Stagrba** → **Stagrdinte** *(the sea spring)* — Auld (northern) land under Meridian (southern) rule, c. 474

## Faiths

### the Rite of Vavomortindo — Vavomortindo, god of the Harvest

*Vavomortindo: the sky blood.*

> Vavomortindo scattered the first seed across Deoda; the golden fields were the gift, and the tithe the price.

Followed across 8 region(s).

### the Cult of Vazizamghim — Vazizamghim, god of the Hearth

*Vazizamghim: the storm sun.*

> Vazizamghim lit the first hearth in Shinsham, and its flame has never since gone out.

Followed across 9 region(s).

### the Rite of Vaveillafjull — Vaveillafjull, god of the Sun

*Vaveillafjull: the sky star.*

> Each dawn Vaveillafjull drives the sun over Mantebeosso; the people of Braba greet it with fire.

Followed across 3 region(s).

## Resources & trade

- **Major exports:** Livestock, Horses, Grain, Fish, Wine
- **Wealthiest settlement:** Nianteliria
- **Trade hubs:** Niantetomor, Nianteliria
- **Deposits:** Iron ×17, Copper ×13, Gold ×5, Gems ×3, Stone ×38, Coal ×17, Timber ×3, Grain ×25, Livestock ×97, Fish ×11, Furs ×1, Spices ×1, Salt ×7, Horses ×32, Wine ×14

## Languages

*Every place-name in this world is a compound of the roots below. Read them and the map reads back.*

### Kesh (desert)

*Spoken in Shinsham, Khirarer, Khirkhidku.*

**kan** water · **khir** sea · **ghash** river · **takish** lake · **kai** spring · **khaim** stone · **mair** mountain · **khidku** hill · **khukha** field · **zash** vale · **zujikh** isle · **sham** cliff · **khash** marsh · **ghen** land · **khajez** wood · **qa** tree · **shid** thorn · **mid** grass · **zajiq** fire · **zair** ash · **kash** ice · **rarer** sand · **kaz** salt · **mish** iron · **rair** gold · **ja** high · **qish** deep · **shaim** dark · **me** bright · **kaar** old · **ki** new · **qu** red · **kheq** white · **ran** black · **ghush** green · **tu** cold · **shin** wild · **shu** fort · **makh** hall · **ghekh** gate · **zaa** bridge · **kad** haven · **qe** home · **riz** market · **shaa** folk · **maad** king · **said** blood · **kaasi** war · **taitan** peace · **ju** holy · **qidka** dread · **ghair** dawn · **ghisaa** dusk · **sagha** star · **shad** moon · **ghim** sun · **ghaza** sky · **rikhaa** wind · **zizam** storm

### Meridian (southern)

*Spoken in Deoda, Beossocaria, Beossoriando.*

**milen** water · **deo** sea · **bella** river · **si** lake · **dinte** spring · **neosso** stone · **beosso** mountain · **cina** hill · **pana** field · **riando** vale · **doria** isle · **laria** cliff · **po** marsh · **caria** land · **veolla** wood · **baria** tree · **sena** thorn · **peona** grass · **comor** fire · **le** ash · **vi** ice · **da** sand · **maria** salt · **re** iron · **taria** gold · **rauria** high · **sauna** deep · **semor** dark · **peoria** bright · **cau** old · **belen** new · **vasso** red · **mante** white · **dona** black · **niante** green · **vau** cold · **varia** wild · **tomor** fort · **lamor** hall · **ciamor** gate · **nelen** bridge · **liria** haven · **demor** home · **telen** market · **binte** folk · **na** king · **tindo** blood · **meonte** war · **dente** peace · **tena** holy · **losso** dread · **mau** dawn · **bamor** dusk · **selen** star · **raulla** moon · **rondo** sun · **vomor** sky · **lolla** wind · **momor** storm

### Auld (northern)

*Spoken in Braba, Stagrbei, Brasturn.*

**vyvask** water · **stagr** sea · **hra** river · **grei** lake · **ba** spring · **bry** stone · **bra** mountain · **hrei** hill · **fjan** field · **thaugr** vale · **stausk** isle · **sturn** cliff · **brein** marsh · **kno** land · **bei** wood · **gro** tree · **gra** thorn · **thaurn** grass · **thygr** fire · **vu** ash · **staun** ice · **knaurk** sand · **than** salt · **tharn** iron · **steirk** gold · **fjeisk** high · **knyst** deep · **thatho** dark · **knausk** bright · **vyn** old · **fjaun** new · **breist** red · **stork** white · **do** black · **von** green · **ska** cold · **knyrk** wild · **thu** fort · **hro** hall · **bust** gate · **grull** bridge · **dund** haven · **fjysk** home · **gry** market · **daurk** folk · **stall** king · **hryrk** blood · **skei** war · **thon** peace · **thork** holy · **brogr** dread · **thost** dawn · **kneirk** dusk · **fjull** star · **va** moon · **vyrk** sun · **veill** sky · **skyva** wind · **voll** storm

## Sagas of the Peoples

*One saga per culture — the founding as its own singers keep it. The facts are the world's facts; the pride is their own.*

### The Saga of Rairghen (Kesh (desert))

> Long the desert folk wandered the singing sands,
> and the shore they found they would not leave.
> Shinsham they named it — the wild cliff —
> a name that fit the land like weather.
> Khirkad they built first, the sea haven,
> its harbour open like a hand.
> (The maps write it Khirtomor now. The saga does not.)
> Over it they set Vazizamghim, the storm sun,
> whose dominion is the hearth;
> and the Cult of Vazizamghim remembers.
> In their tongue, zajiq is fire and qe is home;
> so read their map: every name a small song.
> Of Rairghen their singers made much, once.
> It rose to 2 provinces and it fell, and the saga does not say so —
> but the chronicle does.

### The Saga of Rauriabinte (Meridian (southern))

> Long the southern folk wandered the warm sea,
> and where the water ended, so did the wandering.
> Deoda they named it — the sea sand —
> for they were a people who named things truly.
> Niantetomor they built first, the green fort,
> its quays the first words of a long argument with the sea.
> Over it they set Vavomortindo, the sky blood,
> whose dominion is the harvest;
> and the Rite of Vavomortindo keeps that fire yet.
> In their tongue, comor is fire and selen is star;
> so read their map: every name a small song.
> And of Rauriabinte the singers still sing,
> for its banners stand as high as ever they did.

### The Saga of Storkathon (Auld (northern))

> Out of the grey water came the northern folk,
> and here the road wore out before the people did.
> Braba they named it — the mountain spring —
> and the name has outlived every wall they raised.
> Stagragrull they built first, the sea bridge,
> its quays the first words of a long argument with the sea.
> (The maps write it Stagrlamor now. The saga does not.)
> Over it they set Vaveillafjull, the sky star,
> whose dominion is the sun;
> and the Rite of Vaveillafjull keeps that fire yet.
> In their tongue, stagr is sea and fjull is star;
> so read their map: every name a small song.
> Of Storkathon their singers made much, once.
> It rose to 1 province and it fell, and the saga does not say so —
> but the chronicle does.

## Legends of the founding age

- **0 A.T.** — In the year now called zero, snow lay a man deep from sea to sea, and those who lived through it dated everything afterwards from the year the thaw came. From that year the peoples of this world count their days.
- **55 A.T.** — The city of Niantetomor was founded on a sheltered harbour, destined to become the seat of power.
- **69 A.T.** — The city of Deotomor was founded on a sheltered harbour.
- **71 A.T.** — Niantetomor rose to rule its hinterland, and the realm of Tariademor was proclaimed.
- **81 A.T.** — Deotomor rose to rule its hinterland, and the realm of Lossocaria was proclaimed.
- **476 A.T.** — Mantebeosso woke in fire and ash; a hard winter followed, and harvests failed across the land.
- **764 A.T.** — A great storm-tide flooded Niantetomor; the lower quarters were lost to the sea.
- **809 A.T.** — Under a long peace, Niantetomor flourished; its libraries and roads became the wonder of the world.
- **971 A.T.** — An academy founded on the shores of Mantemilen gathered the age's finest cartographers.

## Rise and fall of realms

*Simulated 100–1100 after the Thaw. 2 realm(s) endured to the present.*

| Realm | Founded | Peak | Peak year | Final | Fate |
|-------|--------:|-----:|----------:|------:|------|
| Rauriabinte | 100 | 11 | 875 | 11 | ascendant |
| Peoriana | 100 | 10 | 775 | 9 | ascendant |
| Lossocaria | 100 | 6 | 525 | 0 | extinct |
| Tariademor | 100 | 3 | 100 | 0 | extinct |
| Rairghen | 149 | 2 | 149 | 0 | extinct |
| Qidkaghen | 223 | 2 | 223 | 0 | extinct |

## The Chronicle of Rauriabinte

*Set down in Niantetomor, in the year 1100 after the Thaw, by a servant of House Mantetindo. What follows is not legend — of the founding age the legends tell enough — but the plain record of 1000 years, as well as one hand could gather it.*

*The years in these pages are counted after the Thaw; the first of the legends tells how the count began, with the Long Winter.*

*The world it happened in is quickly drawn: Mount Mantebeosso in the high places, and the Knauskba running to the sea, and between them every road this chronicle walks.*

### I. The Settling, 100–300

It was in 106 that Peoriana crossed into Braba, and it did not cross back; Storkathon redrew its maps. Within the year, Kaarshaa held Khirkhidku against Rauriabinte, whose army went home leaner and wiser. Before long, sickness walked through Midrarer and was not particular. Hard on its heels, not all was iron: a golden age dawned over Peoriana, and its cities flourished as never before.

149: the assault Peoriana loosed upon Stagrbei broke against Storkathon's spears and came to nothing. The same year, too big to govern and too proud to shrink, Peoriana watched Midkhaim walk away and become Rairghen. Soon after, what had once broken against Khirkhidku's defences now carried them: Rauriabinte took the province, and Kaarshaa's pride with it. The realm of Kaarshaa was extinguished; its name passes from the roll of the powers.

In the year 174, Storkathon held Stagrbei against Lossocaria, whose army went home leaner and wiser. Peoriana moved against Midkhaim, but the desert realm of Rairghen threw the invaders back over the border. Close on that, Rauriabinte came for Deopeona and left without it; the war songs on both sides insist otherwise.

222: Storkathon held Stagrbei against Lossocaria, whose army went home leaner and wiser. Nor was that all: whatever Tariademor intended for Shinsham, Rairghen had prepared for it twice over. Hard on its heels, Peoriana had grown past its own strength: Midshaa and its neighbours broke away to found Qidkaghen. Within the year, the physicians of Beossocaria tried everything; the gravediggers finished what was left.

Peoriana took Beossocaria in 246, and the desert realm of Qidkaghen could do little but record the loss. And in that year too, what had once broken against Deopeona's defences now carried them: Rauriabinte took the province, and Tariademor's pride with it. Hard on its heels, sickness walked through Shinrarer and was not particular. Soon after, the matter of Midshaa was settled with spears — Rauriabinte kept the province, Qidkaghen the grievance.

271: so ended Qidkaghen, whose banners had flown for 48 years. Before long, the people of Beossocaria drove out Peoriana's governors and took for themselves the name Rena. Hard on its heels, Midrarer cracked and browned under a sky that owed it water and paid none. It was Rauriabinte's year: Beossocaria was taken from Rena before the passes closed. It was the end of Rena: 26 years of banners, done in a single season.

About the year 299, Kaarkaasi left Lossocaria the way grown children leave — with Jakhaim, and without asking. That same year, these were the high years of Rauriabinte — its markets loud, its walls, for a while, unneeded.

The map grew fuller meanwhile: 4 new settlements took root, Deotomor first among them.

The occupiers kept the land-words and changed the rest, and so Khirkhukha is written Khirpana in every ledger since — and 3 other names with it.

### II. The Contested Years, 300–500

In 303, the matter of Jakhaim was settled with spears — Peoriana kept the province, Kaarkaasi the grievance. Soon after, the maps needed redrawing that year: Kaarkaasi was gone from them. Before long, Peoriana taxed Jakhaim once too often; the realm of Jukaasi was the receipt.

In the year 319, the shrines of the Cult of Vazizamghim in Khirzash emptied, and those of the Rite of Vavomortindo filled. In the same season, a great drought lay upon Pananeosso; the rivers shrank and the wells soured. By 346, Rauriabinte and Tariademor met over Pananeosso yet again, and yet again it was Rauriabinte that kept the field. It was soon after that Lossocaria crossed into Jakhaim, and it did not cross back; Jukaasi redrew its maps. With that, the realm of Jukaasi was extinguished; its name passes from the roll of the powers.

About the year 349, the people of Pananeosso drove out Rauriabinte's governors and took for themselves the name Rauriana. Soon after, there was no halting Rauriabinte that season: Pananeosso fell to it while Rauriana looked on. It was the end of Rauriana: 17 years of banners, done in a single season.

In the year 374, the priests of the Rite of Vavomortindo out-argued the priests of the Cult of Vazizamghim in Shinrarer, which is how those wars are won. A pestilence swept Jakhaim and carried off more than any war had. Before long, the old quarrel between Rauriabinte and Tariademor found its old battlefield: Shinrarer changed hands once more, in the usual direction. Hard on its heels, of Tariademor nothing more is written.

About the year 411, what had once broken against Stagrbei's defences now carried them: Peoriana took the province, and Storkathon's pride with it. The maps needed redrawing that year: Storkathon was gone from them. And in that year too, one more province, Shinsham, joined Rauriabinte's list; Rairghen's name joined another. In the same season, Pananeosso rose against Rauriabinte, and out of the rising was proclaimed the free realm of Tariabinte.

In 424, quieter conquests were made as well: Mairkhidku turned from the Rite of Vaveillafjull to the Rite of Vavomortindo. Rauriabinte seized Pananeosso from Tariabinte, and the border stones were moved in the night. And that was the whole of Tariabinte's ending — no siege worth the name, no song; a surrender and a seal. Within the year, Peoriana came for Midkhaim and left without it; the war songs on both sides insist otherwise.

About the year 449, the physicians of Jakhaim tried everything; the gravediggers finished what was left. Whatever Rauriabinte intended for Midkhaim, Rairghen had prepared for it twice over. No rain came to Shinrarer for two years together; many perished. I set it down as it was told to me.

In 491, what had once broken against Midkhaim's defences now carried them: Peoriana took the province, and Rairghen's pride with it. The realm of Rairghen was extinguished; its name passes from the roll of the powers.

All the while the land was filling: 4 settlements were raised in these years, Stagragrull and Deodemor among them.

Conquest re-said the very map: Stagragrull became Stagrlamor on its new masters' tongues — and 1 other name with it.

### III. The Marching Years, 500–700

The year 507 opened with trouble: Mairkhidku held — Lossocaria met Peoriana at the border and sent it back the way it came. Close on that, whatever Rauriabinte intended for Midrarer, Lossocaria had prepared for it twice over. Soon after, the people of Midkhaim drove out Peoriana's governors and took for themselves the name Meqe. A generation on, Lossocaria marched on Midkhaim, and by winter the province had fallen to it; the young desert realm of Meqe was the poorer. With that, the realm of Meqe was extinguished; its name passes from the roll of the powers. The record here is thin, and I have not thickened it.

About the year 548, sickness walked through Deopeona and was not particular. Within the year, what had once broken against Mairkhidku's defences now carried them: Peoriana took the province, and Lossocaria's pride with it. Close on that, Rauriabinte moved against Midkhaim, but Lossocaria threw the invaders back over the border.

In the year 568, word came from Mairkhidku that it would be governed from within: thus Rairkaasi, born in a single furious season. Before long, the appetite of Peoriana was becoming proverbial: Mairkhidku this time, and Rairkaasi unable to answer. So ended Rairkaasi, whose banners had flown for 29 years.

About the year 612, Lossocaria held Jakhaim against Peoriana, whose army went home leaner and wiser. Hard on its heels, Rauriabinte came for Midkhaim and left without it; the war songs on both sides insist otherwise. Before long, the physicians of Shinrarer tried everything; the gravediggers finished what was left.

The year 629 opened with trouble: Ghashakhukha emptied the way a bowl empties — slowly, and then all at once. Soon after, Lossocaria moved against Mairkhidku, but Peoriana threw the invaders back over the border. Before long, a pestilence swept Peonariando and carried off more than any war had.

In the year 661, what had once broken against Deoriando's defences now carried them: Peoriana took the province, and Lossocaria's pride with it. Peoriana and Lossocaria met over Jakhaim yet again, and yet again it was Peoriana that kept the field.

The map grew fuller meanwhile: 4 new settlements took root, Peonatomor first among them.

### IV. The Age of Blood and Banners, 700–900

About the year 716, the assault Peoriana loosed upon Khirzash broke against Lossocaria's spears and came to nothing. Soon after, what had once broken against Midkhaim's defences now carried them: Rauriabinte took the province, and Lossocaria's pride with it. That same year, Deoriando cracked and browned under a sky that owed it water and paid none.

In 763, what had once broken against Khirzash's defences now carried them: Peoriana took the province, and Lossocaria's pride with it. Within the year, whatever Rauriabinte intended for Midrarer, Lossocaria had prepared for it twice over. Close on that, no rain came to Pananeosso for two years together; many perished. Soon after, the old quarrel between Peoriana and Lossocaria found its old battlefield: Midrarer changed hands once more, in the usual direction.

In 794, the maps needed redrawing that year: Lossocaria was gone from them. Peoriana moved against Beossocaria, but Rauriabinte threw the invaders back over the border. Close on that, whatever Rauriabinte intended for Beossoriando, Peoriana had prepared for it twice over. Hard on its heels, sickness walked through Pananeosso and was not particular.

865: Peoriana came for Beossocaria and left without it; the war songs on both sides insist otherwise. In the years that followed, what had once broken against Beossoriando's defences now carried them: Rauriabinte took the province, and Peoriana's pride with it.

### V. The Age of Iron, 900–1100

The year 919 opened with trouble: Peoriana and Rauriabinte met over Beossocaria yet again, and yet again it was Peoriana that kept the field. Within the year, whatever Rauriabinte intended for Brasturn, Peoriana had prepared for it twice over. By 954, the assault Rauriabinte loosed upon Beossocaria broke against Peoriana's spears and came to nothing.

In 1014, what had once broken against Beossocaria's defences now carried them: Rauriabinte took the province, and Peoriana's pride with it. In the years that followed, Peoriana came for Beossocaria and left without it; the war songs on both sides insist otherwise. Close on that, Brasturn held — Peoriana met Rauriabinte at the border and sent it back the way it came. Whatever Peoriana intended for Beossocaria, Rauriabinte had prepared for it twice over.

The year 1083 opened with trouble: the priests of the Rite of Vavomortindo out-argued the priests of the Cult of Vazizamghim in Beossoriando, which is how those wars are won. I set it down as it was told to me.

*So the matter stands in 1100: 2 realms endure, and Rauriabinte is mightiest among them, holding 11 provinces. 1 town lies in ruins; their names are kept in this book, which is more than their walls kept.*

*Saunai Vassosena reigns in Niantetomor. Let whoever comes after me write the next page better than I have written this one.*

## A Traveller's Account, in the year 1100

*I, Tindir, a buyer of horses and rumours, set out from Niantetomor in the spring of 1100 to see how much of this country a pair of boots could hold. What follows is the road as I found it — no more, and I hope no less.*

Deotelen lies 15 leagues on from Niantetomor, through grass as high as the stirrup. We forded twice; I stopped counting wet boots. The road climbs some 700 metres before it thinks better of it. Deotelen itself — the sea market — is a town with its face to the sea; its market is all grain and livestock.

From Deotelen the road runs 10 leagues to Khirtomor, through more of the same. We forded once, boots held high. Khirtomor itself — the sea fort — is a town with its face to the sea; its market is all livestock and fish. The people here still say Khirkad; the ledgers say Khirtomor. I bought bread in both names.

I went back down my own road as far as Niantetomor, then turned for Peonatomor. We forded twice; I stopped counting wet boots. Peonatomor itself — the grass fort — is a village; its market is all livestock and grain.

Out of Peonatomor, then, toward Midliria, through more of the same. We forded twice; I stopped counting wet boots. Midliria itself — the grass haven — is a village; its market is all livestock and fish. The people here still say Midshu; the ledgers say Midliria. I bought bread in both names.

From Midliria the road runs 37 leagues to Deonelen, across bare rock where the wind never rests. The way crosses Beossocaria. There is one ford, cold but honest. The road climbs some 3250 metres before it thinks better of it. Deonelen itself — the sea bridge — is a village with its face to the sea; its market is all fish and livestock.

Deodemor lies 12 leagues on from Deonelen, through gold grass and scattered trees. The road climbs some 700 metres before it thinks better of it. Deodemor itself — the sea home — is a village with its face to the sea; its market is all livestock and stone.

Nianteliria lies 11 leagues on from Deodemor, through grass as high as the stirrup. We forded once, boots held high. The road climbs some 350 metres before it thinks better of it. Nianteliria itself — the green haven — is a town with its face to the sea; its market is all grain and livestock.

The Stagrdinte road leaves Nianteliria by the short way, through more of the same. The road finds water twice on this stretch. The road climbs some 450 metres before it thinks better of it. Stagrdinte itself — the sea spring — is a village with its face to the sea; its market is all grain and horses. The people here still say Stagrba; the ledgers say Stagrdinte. I bought bread in both names.

Two days' walking took me from Stagrdinte to Stagrlamor. We forded once, boots held high. The road climbs some 550 metres before it thinks better of it. Stagrlamor itself — the sea hall — is a town with its face to the sea; its market is all livestock and horses. The people here still say Stagragrull; the ledgers say Stagrlamor. I bought bread in both names.

Deotomor lies 11 leagues on from Stagrlamor, through more of the same. We forded once, boots held high. Deotomor itself — the sea fort — is a city with its face to the sea; its market is all wine and grain.

Two days' walking took me from Deotomor to Khirpana, through gold grass and scattered trees. We forded twice; I stopped counting wet boots. Khirpana itself — the sea field — is a village with its face to the sea; its market is all livestock and horses. The people here still say Khirkhukha; the ledgers say Khirpana. I bought bread in both names.

*I came home by the same roads, which were somehow longer in that direction. Tindir, 1100.*

## Heightmap

A real 360×360 16-bit heightmap is exported alongside this report (`.heightmap16.png` and raw `.heightmap.r16`). Import it into Blender, Unity, Godot, or World Machine and scale the height to **5,600 m** (value 65535 = 5,600 m above the lowest point). The topographic contour map (`.topo.png`) shows the same relief as isolines.
